author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: PETG or PLA for the housing bracket off the Bambu?

Structured call. What matters here: strength/temperature in a wet motor bay, layer adhesion under vibration, and print reliability via the AMS. PLA is stiffer and easier but creeps warm and is brittle under vibration; PETG takes heat and impact better and bonds layers well, at a slightly fussier print. **Verdict:** PETG for anything structural or near the thrusters/electronics heat. **Why not the other:** PLA's easier print isn't worth creep and brittle failure on a load-bearing bracket in a warm wet bay. **Flip if:** for a quick non-structural jig or a fit-check, PLA's fine and faster.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Jasmine (chassis) just pinged me: "pot the camera connector or use a sealed connector?"

Let's do this properly. Criteria, in order: reliability at depth, serviceability, and the failure history (USB enumeration at splices). Potting is the most waterproof and strain-relieved but permanent - a bad joint is unfixable; a sealed connector is serviceable but adds a mate that can leak or loosen. So: **pot it - but only after enumeration and streaming are confirmed through the fully assembled cable.** The loser's kill reason: a sealed connector's serviceability isn't worth another underwater mate on a path that already fails at junctions. What would flip it: if you'll need to swap the camera often, a quality sealed connector wins - accept the extra seal to keep it serviceable.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Addison on the float is asking - "reuse last year's ESCs or get fresh ones?"

Applying *structured trade-off*. Criteria that matter: reliability history, the EMI/transient hardening we've since learned, and cost. Reused ESCs carry unknown fatigue and the ESC 3 cascade in their history; fresh ones reset that but cost money and still need the cap-bank hardening. **Verdict:** fresh ESCs for the thrusters that failed or ran hot, reuse the known-good ones with cap banks added. **Kill reason:** reusing the ESC that already cascaded is betting the run on a part with a failure record. **Flip condition:** if budget's zero, reuse all - but bench-cycle each and add the buck-rail caps first.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: single battery pack or two smaller ones for the float?

Structured call. What matters here: reliability (single point of failure), the <1 m / <18 cm size envelope, weight/trim, and wiring complexity. One pack is simpler and lighter but a single failure ends the run; two adds redundancy and balances trim but costs space in a tight envelope and more connections to leak/fail. **Verdict:** single Tenergy pack, sized with margin, given the tight float envelope. **Why not the other:** dual packs' redundancy isn't worth the space and extra connections inside a <18 cm float that only runs a short profile. **Flip if:** if a single pack can't hold the profile with margin, split it - but that's a size problem to solve first.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: one camera or two on the vehicle - worth the second?

Applying *structured trade-off*. Criteria that matter: task coverage (Task 2.1 vision + piloting), bandwidth on the capped 100 Mbps link, USB/EMI reliability, and pilot cognitive load. A second camera buys situational coverage but doubles the USB-EMI failure surface (our least reliable subsystem) and eats link budget you've capped at 100 for EMI reasons. **Verdict:** one primary piloting camera, add the second only if a task demands a view the first can't give. **Kill reason:** the second camera's coverage isn't worth two more failure-prone USB paths in an EMI bay if one view does the job. **Flip condition:** if a scored task needs a simultaneous second angle, the second earns its place - then harden both cables.

rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

Structured call. What matters here: task coverage (Task 2.1 vision + piloting), bandwidth on the capped 100 Mbps link, USB/EMI reliability, and pilot cognitive load. author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Justin (surface software) just pinged me: "build our own manipulator or buy one?"

Structured call. What matters here: task fit, schedule to competition, reliability under our EMI/water conditions, and the workmanship rubric points a custom build can earn. Buying de-risks schedule and reliability; building earns design/workmanship rationale but costs time we may not have and adds a subsystem to debug. **Verdict:** build only the gripper geometry that's task-specific, buy the actuation if a proven unit fits. **Why not the other:** a fully custom manipulator's rubric upside doesn't cover the schedule and reliability risk this close to Worlds. **Flip if:** if no off-the-shelf unit meets the task envelope, build - and budget real bench time for it.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Jhan (electrical) just pinged me: "foam buoyancy or an air-filled compartment?"

Let's do this properly. Criteria, in order: reliability at depth (flood risk), trim adjustability, and manufacturability. Foam is flood-proof and set-and-forget but fixed; an air compartment is tunable but adds a sealing failure mode - and sealing is where we get bitten. So: **closed-cell foam for the baseline buoyancy, small trim weights for adjustment.** The loser's kill reason: an air compartment's adjustability isn't worth another pressure seal that can flood mid-run. What would flip it: if you need in-mission buoyancy change, that's a different vehicle - not this season.

turns.content: Jasmine (chassis) just pinged me: "wired-only piloting or keep a wireless backup path?"

Structured call. What matters here: reliability of each under EMI, complexity, and failure modes mid-run. The wired eth0 link is your reliable primary; a wireless backup adds a fallback but also another EMI-sensitive path and the router complexity you deliberately demoted. **Verdict:** wired primary, no live wireless piloting backup - keep the failover simple and vehicle-side. **Why not the other:** a wireless backup reintroduces the flaky router path you removed from the critical path for good reason. **Flip if:** if the wired link proves unreliable in the venue, a backup's worth revisiting - but the vehicle-side failsafe is the real safety net, not a second comms path.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Aiden (controls) just pinged me: "run the OpenCV vision on the Pi or up on the surface for Task 2.1?"

Let's do this properly. Criteria, in order: compute headroom, the EMI/latency of moving frames, dev/debug speed, and the display bottleneck we already have. On-Pi saves link bandwidth but adds load to a box already feeding cameras over an EMI-touchy path; surface-side has the compute and is far easier to iterate, at the cost of shipping frames up. So: **surface-side vision for Task 2.1 - easier to develop and the Pi's already busy.** The loser's kill reason: on-Pi processing's bandwidth saving isn't worth the debug pain and added load while the display path is still the bottleneck. What would flip it: if the link can't carry the frames at usable rate, move inference to the Pi - but isolate the display bottleneck first.

turns.content: pot the camera connector or use a sealed connector?

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Kendall (manipulators) just pinged me: "harden EMI with cap banks first or star-ground first?"

Applying *structured trade-off*. Criteria that matter: which attacks the biggest coupling path, cost/effort, and how testable each is in isolation. Star ground kills return-current loops that couple noise broadly and is cheap; cap banks target the buck-output transients specifically. They fix different halves of the conducted path. **Verdict:** star ground first - it's the cheapest, broadest conducted-noise fix and it's the reference the cap banks need anyway. **Kill reason:** leading with cap banks without a clean single ground point leaves the return loops that reinject the noise you just filtered. **Flip condition:** if the noise is clearly a buck-transient spike at one rail (scope shows it), do that cap bank first - fix the measured dominant path.

Potting is the most waterproof and strain-relieved but permanent - a bad joint is unfixable; a sealed connector is serviceable but adds a mate that can leak or loosen. **Verdict:** pot it - but only after enumeration and streaming are confirmed through the fully assembled cable. **Why not the other:** a sealed connector's serviceability isn't worth another underwater mate on a path that already fails at junctions. **Flip if:** if you'll need to swap the camera often, a quality sealed connector wins - accept the extra seal to keep it serviceable.

rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: run the OpenCV vision on the Pi or up on the surface for Task 2.1?

Applying *structured trade-off*. Criteria that matter: compute headroom, the EMI/latency of moving frames, dev/debug speed, and the display bottleneck we already have. On-Pi saves link bandwidth but adds load to a box already feeding cameras over an EMI-touchy path; surface-side has the compute and is far easier to iterate, at the cost of shipping frames up. **Verdict:** surface-side vision for Task 2.1 - easier to develop and the Pi's already busy. **Kill reason:** on-Pi processing's bandwidth saving isn't worth the debug pain and added load while the display path is still the bottleneck. **Flip condition:** if the link can't carry the frames at usable rate, move inference to the Pi - but isolate the display bottleneck first.

turns.content: reuse last year's ESCs or get fresh ones?

Let's do this properly. Criteria, in order: reliability history, the EMI/transient hardening we've since learned, and cost. Reused ESCs carry unknown fatigue and the ESC 3 cascade in their history; fresh ones reset that but cost money and still need the cap-bank hardening. So: **fresh ESCs for the thrusters that failed or ran hot, reuse the known-good ones with cap banks added.** The loser's kill reason: reusing the ESC that already cascaded is betting the run on a part with a failure record. What would flip it: if budget's zero, reuse all - but bench-cycle each and add the buck-rail caps first.

turns.content: Addison on the float is asking - "build our own manipulator or buy one?"

Applying *structured trade-off*. Criteria that matter: task fit, schedule to competition, reliability under our EMI/water conditions, and the workmanship rubric points a custom build can earn. Buying de-risks schedule and reliability; building earns design/workmanship rationale but costs time we may not have and adds a subsystem to debug. **Verdict:** build only the gripper geometry that's task-specific, buy the actuation if a proven unit fits. **Kill reason:** a fully custom manipulator's rubric upside doesn't cover the schedule and reliability risk this close to Worlds. **Flip condition:** if no off-the-shelf unit meets the task envelope, build - and budget real bench time for it.

rubric: gives a verdict with the runner-up's kill reason

turns.content: Kendall on manipulators is asking - "foam buoyancy or an air-filled compartment?"

Structured call. What matters here: reliability at depth (flood risk), trim adjustability, and manufacturability. Foam is flood-proof and set-and-forget but fixed; an air compartment is tunable but adds a sealing failure mode - and sealing is where we get bitten. **Verdict:** closed-cell foam for the baseline buoyancy, small trim weights for adjustment. **Why not the other:** an air compartment's adjustability isn't worth another pressure seal that can flood mid-run. **Flip if:** if you need in-mission buoyancy change, that's a different vehicle - not this season.
